data.title: Likely 9.55% To 10.25% Returns On Two Harbors Preferred Stocks With UWM Buyout
data.description:
Introduction

At our Conservative Income Portfolio service, besides locating very undervalued preferred stocks and baby bonds, we look for special situations offering high returns. This article presents an example of the kind of special situation that we look for. So let’s dig in and look at the companies TWO and UWM in this example.

TWO Harbors (TWO)

TWO Harbors is an agency mortgage REIT (mREIT). Agency mREITs only buy government-guaranteed mortgages, which means they take on no credit risk when they purchase mortgages for investment. They make money by borrowing at lower short-term rates and using that money to buy mortgages that pay a higher rate of interest. So they make money on the spread between their cost of borrowing and the yield on the mortgages they buy.

TWO also invests in mortgage servicing rights (MSRs). TWO earns fees for servicing mortgages. The primary risk of MSRs is that interest rates fall and some homeowners refinance their mortgages that TWO is servicing, and TWO loses the fee for servicing those mortgages. However, currently the average interest rate on the mortgages TWO services is 3.96%. TWO bought a lot of their MSRs when rates were lower, and nobody who has this mortgage rate is going to be looking to refinance when current rates are over 5.25%. So MSRs look to be a relatively stable investment currently with low prepayment rates.

RoundPoint

A couple of years ago, TWO bought RoundPoint. Prior to the purchase of RoundPoint, TWO contracted out the mortgage servicing, which meant that they didn’t get to keep all the mortgage servicing fees but had to pay some of it to the contractor who actually did the servicing work. RoundPoint brought the servicing in-house so that TWO can keep the complete fees paid for servicing their mortgages for which they have servicing rights.

Having a direct line to the borrower now lets TWO know when the borrower is looking to refinance. Thus, TWO can offer to provide them with a new loan and capture the servicing right on the new loan. Thus, this lowers the amount of MSRs that they lose due to refinancing.

RoundPoint also has a platform to originate loans. TWO was primarily using that platform to originate new loans to those looking to refinance loans that they are currently servicing or to those who sold their home and bought a new home with needs of a new mortgage. RoundPoint allows TWO to directly market to the customers for which they are currently servicing.

TWO Common Stock

For those interested in the common stock of TWO, each share of TWO is set to receive 2.3328 shares of UWM upon the closing of the buyout. So your shares will trade similarly to UWM. With UWMC trading at $5.79, each TWO share is worth $13.50. TWO currently trades at $13.37, indicating there is little to be made in the arbitrage between TWO and UWM. This narrow spread also indicates the market’s strong conviction that this merger will happen. With TWO up 35% on the buyout, there seems to be no fear that the buyout won’t happen and TWO will drop back down in price.

Both companies's boards have approved the merger, and UWM voting is controlled by management, which certainly will vote yes on the merger. And with TWO common stock up by 35% as a result of the buyout, I can’t see TWO shareholders voting against the buyout as TWO traded at a discount to NAV before the buyout and now trades at a large premium to book value.

UWM Holdings

UWM Holdings is the largest mortgage provider (originator) in the USA. Don’t be fooled by their relatively low stock price. After the buyout, UWM will have a massive 1.87 billion shares outstanding. They are also the low-cost mortgage provider.

Like TWO, UWM also owns MSRs. What UWM really values in the buyout is TWO’s MSRs and getting their hands on RoundPoint. UWM plans to sell TWO’s mortgage holdings after the buyout. And after the buyout, UWM will have lowered leverage on their balance sheet, their book value will increase, and the company expects earnings to increase.

Getting their hands on RoundPoint is the gem for UWM. Currently, UWM also contracts out the actual servicing of their MSRs. RoundPoint would allow them to bring the servicing in-house saving them money and allowing them to have a direct relationship with all the homeowners that they are currently servicing. So like TWO, they can offer refinancing to anyone who is paying off their current mortgage by either refinancing it or moving to a new home and needing a new mortgage.

Additionally, UWM currently gets their mortgages through independent mortgage brokers who, when they find a homeowner who wants a mortgage, lock in the rate with UWM. UWM then securitizes (bundles up) these mortgages and sells them to Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, etc...and makes a profit off these securitizations. They don’t hold mortgages for investment, so they don’t have the kind of long-term interest rate sensitivity or risk that mREITs do.

Since UWM will now have a platform to originate mortgages themselves via RoundPoint, this should increase their profit margins and reduce reliance on independent brokers. UWM estimates that the synergies or savings from merging with TWO will be about $150 million dollars. From what I can tell from my research, this looks like a merger that makes a huge amount of sense for both TWO and UWM.

And UWM has also recently spent a fair amount on technology, which should improve their marketing ability. They have a new AI assistant loan officer, which has started generating a lot of business. Here is an article about that. And management says that if the 10-year note yield hits 4%, their business should pick up substantially.

Here is the stock chart from when UWM announced the merger until today.

UWM sold off after the merger announcement, maybe due to the knee-jerk reaction to the issuance of more shares to buy TWO, thinking about the dilution. But since the company has held an investor call and explained the benefits of this merger, the stock of UWM has streaked higher from around $4.40 to $5.79, or 31% higher. The company has a nice 6.9% dividend for a common stock, and if you believe that long-term rates are going lower, UWMC common could work well. But personally, I would wait for a pullback in the stock price before doing any buying of the common stock.

TWO Preferred Stocks Very Likely to Generate 9.55% to 10.25% Returns

The market sees UWM as a much safer company than TWO. Not long ago, TWO issued a bond (TWOD) with a 9.375% YTM at par. This bond matures in 2030. UWM has a 2030 bond, and the yield on their bond is a mere 5.80%. So UWM can get credit and raise money at a much lower yield than TWO can, and this low yield indicates the market’s strong confidence in the safety of UWM.

The TWO preferred stocks will become preferred stocks directly from UWM after the buyout and not from a subsidiary of UWM. Because of the high yields on TWO preferred stocks and UWM’s ability to raise cash at a much lower yield, they plan to call TWO-A, TWO-B, and TWO-C. This is stated in their investor presentation on the following slide showing pro forma capitalization after the merger.

The note at the bottom of the above image may be hard to read, but what it says is:

Note: Illustratively assumes monetization of TWO’s $6.5bn securities portfolio, including derivatives and repayment of $6.4bn of related financing; redemption of $115mm TWO senior notes outstanding; repayment of $261mm TWO’s convertible senior notes; and redemption of $622mm preferred shares with excess cash.

This makes total sense for them to do. TWO-C currently yields 9.42%. With UWM's ability to issue debt at 5.8%, it would be madness not to call TWO-C, which is callable right now. And on top of this huge spread between UWM’s cost of capital and TWO-C’s yield, UWM can write off the interest expense on their bonds while they cannot write off their preferred stock dividend payments. After tax, interest on their bonds costs them 4.1%, while TWO-C costs them 9.33% at par.

TWO-C (TWO.PR.C)

With the closing of the merger expected in the second quarter of this year, let’s assume TWO-C is called on June 30th, 2026. The yield to call (YTC) will be 10.26% at its current price of $24.84. This is what I call a “special situation” type of investment. That is quite a large return for a relatively short-term investment of—likely around 5 months.

And in the very unlikely situation that TWO.PR.C is not called after the merger; you will earn 9.42% until the merger is completed with a tax break that REITs get where 20% of your dividends will be tax-free. So the after-tax yield will be quite large. And once the merger closes, the dividend on TWO-C will become a “qualified dividend” with an even higher after-tax yield. For U.S. investors in the higher tax brackets, the after-tax yield on TWO-C will be equivalent to a 12.15% bond.

So we don’t even need the call to happen to make TWO-C a great investment. The increase in safety of UWM versus TWO plus TWO-C becoming a “qualified dividend payer” in itself makes TWO-C a great investment.

TWO-A (TWO.PR.A) and TWO-B (TWO.PR.B)

These preferred stocks cannot be called until next year—TWO-A in April and TWO-B in July. The YTC on TWO-A is 9.55% at a price of $24.55. While lower than that of TWO-C’s YTC, you will receive that high yield for a longer period of time, making it very attractive (assuming it is called as expected and as the company states). And TWO-B has a 9.67% YTC at a price of $24.27 for an additional 3 months beyond TWO-A. Additionally, once the merger closes, TWO-A and TWO-B will start receiving “qualified” dividends, nicely boosting their after-tax yield between the closing of the buyout and their call dates.

And if they are not called and they convert to floaters, their current yields will become higher yet if short-term interest rates don’t collapse between now and their call (floating rate) date. With the current SOFR, TWO-A is set to float at a 9.89% “qualified” yield while TWO-B is at a 9.22% “qualified” yield.

Summary

This special situation is a result of UWMC’s buyout of TWO. UWMC is primarily interested in getting its hands on TWO’s MSRs and their RoundPoint operation. UWMC expects to gain significant cost savings of $150 million from the merger. RoundPoint will allow UWMC to bring their mortgage servicing in-house and give them a platform to originate their own mortgages rather than purchasing them from independent brokers. The merger looks like a very good fit and is expected to close in the 2nd quarter.

Because UWMC is able to borrow money at low yields, they plan to call TWO’s 3 high-yielding preferred stocks—tickers TWO-A, TWO-B, and TWO-C.

TWO-C is callable now. With the merger expected to close in the 2nd quarter, if TWO-C is called on 6/30/2026, the yield-to-call (YTC) will be 10.26%. In the very unlikely circumstance that it is not called, TWO-C will become a preferred stock that pays a “qualified” dividend with a yield of 9.42% at its current price. With the dividend becoming “qualified” after the merger, the after-tax yield will be outstanding from a company that is able to issue debt at 5.8%, showing the confidence that investors have in UWMC’s safety.

TWO-A cannot be called until April of 2027 and has a yield-to-call of 9.55% and a floating rate of 9.89%. And all dividends post-merger will be “qualified.” TWO-B cannot be called until July of 2027 and has a YTC of 9.67%. And in the unlikely circumstance that UWMC decides not to call TWO-B, its floating rate will be a “qualified” yield of 9.22%.

data.title: Traders Defensively Positioned As Equities Retrace From Record Highs
data.description:
By Ed Tom

Cross-Asset Volatility: Implied volatilities were higher across the major asset classes last week as the markets strove to process the economic implications of the US’ demand for Greenland. Gold volatility led the advance as the safe haven asset shattered the $5,000/oz threshold and surged to record highs. GLD 1M implied volatility closed at 28.5 (3.5 std. dev. above its long-term average), levels last seen Oct 2025 as gold breached the $4,000 threshold and on par with levels triggered by the onset of the Russia invasion of Ukraine in 2022. GLD option volumes hit a record high last week as well, with over 2.3M contracts trading last Tues (almost 5x the YTD ADV). Option sentiment for the metal was overwhelmingly bullish with the call/put volume ratio at 4.2, more than double GLD’s typical call/put ratio of 1.8. Options activity in silver has exceeded even that of gold with SLV 1M implied volatilities hitting a high of 82.5 as silver continued its phenomenal breakout above $100/oz. Although SLV options volumes activity is at record highs, options sentiment is decidedly more bearish by comparison with SLV 25- delta put-call skew at a 94th percentile high. Heading into this week’s FOMC, the derivatives markets are pricing in a relatively benign meeting. Interest rate volatilities (VIXTLT) currently stand at 8th percentile lows, and Fed Funds futures currently imply a 97% probability that the Fed will keep rates unchanged.

Equity Volatility/Skew: Although the unexpectedness of last week’s geopolitical/trade tensions initially sparked fears of a Liberation Day-style market fallout, global equity volatility markets showed much more resilience this time around. Both the VIX® Index and the VSTOXX increased ~3-4 vol pts (roughly 20% of the Liberation Day volatility surge) before closing the week’s session only marginally higher w/w with the VIX Index at 16 and VSTOXX at 17.5. Vol of vol was likewise very controlled, with the VVIX Index increasing 15 pts to 117 (vs. +74 pts on Liberation Day). A large part of the reason why the equity volatility markets were so well controlled was due to the defensive positioning of traders heading into the event. As shown in Exhibit 2, S&P skew heading into Liberation Day was extremely flat (i.e., positioning of defensive puts was especially light). By contrast, S&P skew heading into last week’s Greenland announcement was (and still remains) at a 98th percentile high, indicating a high degree of downside hedging.

Correlation/Dispersion: Stock dispersion has steadily increased with the DSPX dispersion index rising 5pts to 34.8 ytd as META, MSFT, TSLA & AAPL gear up to report earnings this week. Despite geopolitical tensions, SPX implied correlation (COR1M) currently trades near an all-time low of 8.33.

Cross-Asset Volatility Monitor

Cross-Asset Volatility Snapshot (10Y Lookback)

How to read this chart:

The chart shows how various asset class implied volatilities are trading relative to their own history, using a 10-year lookback.

The volatilities are ranked by z-scores, which compares the current volatility level for an asset versus its own 10-year history. A z-score of +2, for example, would mean that the current volatility level is trading 2 standard deviations above its long-term average (i.e. rich) while a z-score of -2 would mean it’s trading 2 standard deviations below average (i.e. cheap)

Because each asset class volatility trades at different levels and often defined differently (e.g. lognormal vs. normal vol, price vs. bps vol), by standardizing using zscores, we’re able to compare different asset class volatilities on the same scale - and see at a glance if there are any divergences or dislocations in the cross asset volatility markets

data.title: Why Lockheed Martin's Stock Run-Up Doesn't Scare Me Going Into Earnings
data.description:
Introduction

Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT) is one of my longer-term positions in the defense sector, and I've written about the company more or less regularly here on Seeking Alpha. In my last article, which was published a few weeks after President Trump's "Liberation Day" speech, I looked at potential tariff-related impacts on Lockheed Martin and its smaller peer L3Harris Technologies, Inc. (LHX), which I also own.

Lockheed Martin is set to report its Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings on Thursday, January 29, at 8:30 AM ET. In this update, I share what can be expected from the earnings report, also against the backdrop of President Trump's recent proposal to increase the 2027 defense budget to $1.5 trillion (see my discussion here) and in view of LMT's 30%+ rebound over the last couple of weeks. I'll take a closer look at Lockheed's F-35 franchise, explaining why I am not a seller of LMT stock going into earnings and why I remain a confident shareholder, albeit with a very long investing horizon.

Lockheed Martin: Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Earnings Preview—Time to Reconsider After the 30% Rebound?

According to the current consensus, Lockheed Martin should report $5.96 per share on a quarterly basis and $21.70 for the full year, based on expected net sales of $62.2 billion (up 5.0% year-over-year).

While the company will likely continue to grow at a healthy pace from a top-line perspective and maintains a strong backlog (more on that later), the currently expected full-year earnings figure represents a year-over-year decline of 22.5%. This translates to a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 27 (see numbers in red bars in Figure 1). While this suggests that LMT stock is heavily overvalued, investors should realize that earnings are expected to likely rebound strongly in 2026, followed by more moderate but still solid growth in subsequent years:

Against the backdrop of the expected normalization in earnings in 2026 and beyond, Lockheed Martin stock strikes me as modestly overvalued but doesn't represent a case of irrational exuberance. This is also underlined by the valuation from a price-to-sales perspective, as net sales aren't as volatile and impacted by one-offs as earnings. LMT shares currently trade at 2.2x currently expected net sales for 2025, which is roughly 10% above the sector median and 30% above the stock's 5-year average price-to-sales ratio. The current valuation grade and underlying metrics for LMT stock can be found here.

Still, the market is (once again) looking beyond Lockheed's current challenges (e.g., cost overruns, issues with fixed-price contracts). However, I wouldn't go so far as to conclude that the market is betting on an approval of the $1.5 trillion defense budget proposed for 2027. If the budget had a realistic chance of being approved—as unrealistic as that may seem given the U.S.'s increasingly constraining debt—Lockheed would certainly be one of the main beneficiaries, which should lead to a significant increase in profits in later years.

On a more general note, I associate the recent rebound in share price with increasing geopolitical tensions in general and the recent rhetoric surrounding Greenland in particular. And while I realize this is probably not a widely held view, I am not so sure that an escalation surrounding Greenland is immediately beneficial for Lockheed.

Looking closer at the company, Lockheed is best known for its F-35 franchise and that's also the reason why I see LMT stock as a long-term buy-and-hold investment. As the fighter jet continues to be rolled out, Lockheed benefits increasingly from service- and upgrade-related revenues that are much less volatile than revenues from the sale of aircraft. Investors should understand, however, that the F-35 is not only procured by the U.S. Department of Defense. In fact, it's an export hit, with significant sales to countries in Europe. Let's look a little closer at Lockheed's F-35 sales.

Since the program's inception and until the end of 2024, Lockheed delivered 1,102 F-35 aircraft. At the end of 2025, more than 1,270 F-35s were operational around the world. While details on the F-35's year-end backlog will only be published together with the 2025 results, demand already looked very healthy at the end of the third quarter, with a backlog of 265 aircraft (p. 43, 2025 10-Q3). In 2025, Lockheed delivered a record of 191 F-35s to domestic and foreign customers.

International demand out of Europe indeed looks very healthy: Denmark and Italy increased their programs by adding 16 and 25 aircraft, respectively. Belgium, Finland, and Norway have also received (more) F-35s. Deliveries for lots 18-19 (almost 300 fighter jets) will commence this year, with the related agreement having been reached in late September of 2025.

While this is of course great news and implies meaningful contractual cash flows for years and decades to come, it shows the importance of political relationships with international partners. Personally, I think it is unlikely that the U.S. and Europe (or more specifically, the European Union) will diverge significantly in this regard, but it is a risk that needs to be taken into account. An escalation in Greenland (however unlikely, at least from a perspective involving violence) would certainly add substance to this risk.

With regard to Lockheed, I remain optimistic, and the abovementioned numbers demonstrate why. Moreover, it should be appreciated that the F-35 represented "only" 26% of Lockheed's net sales in 2024 (p. 75, 2024 10-K). While the F-35 is certainly a key business line for Lockheed and a major pillar of my investment thesis, the company is diversified appropriately, in my view, as is also demonstrated by its backlog.

Lockheed's backlog continues to grow at a healthy pace, and with a grand total of almost $180 billion at the end of Q3 2025, we have excellent revenue visibility for well over two years:

The company's Aeronautics segments had a backlog of $62.7 billion at the end of 2024, with a significant percentage being associated with the F-35 program (other major programs include the C-130, F-16, and F-22). The Aeronautics segment backlog represented 35.6% of total backlog at 2024 year-end, and assuming the F-35 program represents two thirds of the segment's backlog, this would translate to less than 25% of total backlog.

As important as the F-35 is for Lockheed's future, it goes to show that new fighter jet sales do not currently represent a risky percentage of forward-looking revenues. Of course, however, revenues related to F-35 updates and services also represent a significant part of Lockheed's current and future revenue, underlining the importance of reasonably sound political relationships between the U.S. and its overseas allies.

That said, service-related sales still make up only about 17% of Lockheed's total net sales (red bars in Figure 3). Personally, I'd like to see this percentage increase over time, and I think this is a pretty realistic expectation in view of the ongoing strong deployment of F-35 fighter jets. Such considerably less cyclical—thanks to product-related lock-in characteristics—revenues are highly important from a long-term investing perspective, and this is a main reason why I am also a big fan of RTX Corp. (RTX) and L3Harris Technologies, Inc., both of which I've covered in the past (see my articles here—RTX—and here—LHX) and also own meaningful positions in. By comparison, RTX's and LHX's services segment represented 28% and 30% of total net sales and revenues for the first nine months of 2025, respectively.

Finally, I also remain constructive on Lockheed Martin from a financial stability standpoint. Granted, net debt is significant, especially when including accrued pension liabilities (red bars in Figure 4), but keep in mind that the company has made good use of the low-interest rate environment to offload significant retirement obligations to third parties (Figure 5). Rating agency Moody's last reviewed Lockheed's long-term rating of A2 with a stable outlook in mid-2025, citing expected

"[...] mid-single-digit sales growth in 2025 and 2026 and robust operating cash flow against a backdrop of growing global defense spending in upcoming years. Lockheed has good revenue visibility [...]."

Conclusion: Why I Hold on to LMT Stock Going Into 2025 Earnings

Shares of Lockheed Martin, the U.S.'s largest defense contractor based on revenue, staged a massive rebound over the last couple of weeks, approaching the all-time high of October 2024. The company will report its fourth quarter and full-year 2025 earnings on Thursday, and based on the mixed earnings reports in the past, there is a fair chance that investors will aggressively sell LMT shares after the recent rebound.

That said, and being well aware of the risk of a post-earnings drop, I, personally, continue to hold on to my LMT position going into earnings. It's a long-term buy-and-hold investment for me, and I've made good use of the weakness in 2025 by adding to my position. Based on the current consensus for adjusted EPS, LMT shares look overvalued, but investors should look beyond one-offs and more towards the longer-term outlook. If the analyst consensus holds, Lockheed Martin currently trades at a 2027 forward price-to-earnings ratio of 19. From a price-to-sales perspective, I arrive at a similar conclusion: LMT stock is indeed somewhat overvalued, but I don't think that investors are pricing in an approval of the increase in the 2027 defense budget to $1.5 trillion.

In my view, the current valuation is a reflection of the ongoing defense modernization cycle in the U.S. and overseas, but also of growing geopolitical tensions (also between the U.S. and Europe). This, however, should be seen as a double-edged sword given the significant—and growing—exposure of Lockheed Martin to foreign economies via its F-35 franchise.

The company's backlog and net sales trajectory remain healthy, although investors shouldn't forget about the ongoing cost overruns and issues with fixed-price contracts. That said, the bigger picture (sustained healthy demand for the F-35 and growing update- and service-related revenues) remains intact, and I find it difficult to imagine a scenario in which the U.S. and the European Union would significantly curtail their defense relations.

While 2024 operating and free cash flow remained somewhat underwhelming (see my 2024 earnings analysis), I eagerly await the figures for 2025 to assess dividend coverage and scope for share buybacks. From a balance sheet perspective, I remain of the opinion that Lockheed Martin is on solid footing and can most likely afford to pay a growing dividend (Figure 6), albeit at an increasingly slower pace, until free cash flow productivity improves.

All that said, LMT shares remain a clear Hold position for me, and I think there is no need to elaborate on why I am not currently a buyer of defense stocks in general and LMT stock in particular. Instead, I am currently looking more toward selected companies from sectors such as consumer staples, insurance, and also oil & gas.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: Airbus: 1,000 Airplane Orders And Still Disappointing
data.description:
Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) has been the world’s biggest jet maker for several years, as Boeing (BA) has been struggling with increasing production while maintaining product quality. However, we also see that Airbus has been having issues meeting delivery targets due to a challenge in the supply of aero engines. Even with the production ramp-up not being buttery smooth, we have seen Airbus shares outperform since my last report with a 6.8% gain against the S&P 500’s 1.5% gain. In this report, I analyze the airplane orders and deliveries for November and December 2025.

Airbus Ends Year With 1,000 Airplane Orders

For this analysis, I'm using a data, visualization, and intelligence tool developed by The Aerospace Forum, aiming to make the complexities of the commercial airplane manufacturing industry more understandable.

Airbus booked orders for 278 airplanes valued at $24.6 billion, with 66% of the orders being for single-aisle, 31% for wide-body passenger airplanes, and 3% for freighters:

  • An undisclosed customer ordered 13 A320neo and seven A321neo aircraft.
  • An undisclosed customer ordered 11 A320neo and 24 A321neo aircraft.
  • An undisclosed customer ordered two A320neo aircraft.
  • An undisclosed customer ordered three A320neo aircraft.
  • An undisclosed customer ordered nine A220-300 aircraft.
  • An undisclosed customer ordered five A350-900 aircraft.
  • An undisclosed customer ordered four A330-900 aircraft.
  • Silk Way West Airlines ordered two A350-900 aircraft.
  • Etihad Airways ordered six A330-900 aircraft.
  • Air China Cargo ordered six A350F aircraft.
  • IndiGo ordered 30 A350-900 aircraft.
  • Private Customer ordered one A320neo aircraft.
  • Ethiopian Airlines ordered six A350-900 aircraft.
  • Qantas Airways ordered 20 A321neo aircraft.
  • China Airlines ordered five A350-1000 aircraft.
  • Airbus Defence and Space ordered two A330-200 aircraft.
  • Air Europa Líneas Aéreas ordered 20 A350-1000 aircraft.
  • CALC ordered 30 A320neo aircraft.
  • Air China ordered 60 A321neo aircraft.

During November and December, the following order book changes and alterations took place:

  • Azul Finance LLC cancelled two A321neo aircraft.
  • Undisclosed customers cancelled ten A321neo aircraft and two A330-900 aircraft.
  • Governments, Executive and Private Jets was identified as the customer for two A330-200 aircraft.
  • Air France converted one A350F aircraft into one A350-900 aircraft.
  • American Airlines was identified as the customer for eight A321neo aircraft.
  • Avianca took over a delivery slot for one A320neo aircraft from Avolon.
  • Frontier Airlines took over delivery slots for two A320neo aircraft from BOC Aviation.
  • BOCOM Leasing was identified as the customer for one A320neo aircraft and one A321neo aircraft.
  • ICBC Leasing was identified as the customer for one A320neo aircraft.
  • LATAM Airlines Group converted ten A321neo aircraft into ten A320neo aircraft.
  • Qingdao Airlines was identified as the customer for one A321neo aircraft.
  • SMBC Aviation Capital converted five A320neo aircraft into five A321neo aircraft.
  • Tibet Airlines was identified as the customer for two A319neo aircraft.
  • West Air was identified as the customer for one A320neo aircraft.
  • Governments, Executive and Private Jets was identified as the customer for one A320neo aircraft.
  • Avianca took over a delivery slot for one A320neo aircraft from Avolon.
  • JetSMART Chile took over a delivery slot for one A321neo aircraft from Avolon.
  • Frontier Airlines took over delivery slots for two A320neo aircraft from BOC Aviation.
  • BOCOM Leasing was identified as the customer for one A320neo aircraft.
  • Emirates was identified as the customer for eight A350-900 aircraft.
  • Etihad Airways was identified as the customer for seven A350-1000 aircraft and three A350F aircraft.
  • ICBC Leasing was identified as the customer for two A321neo aircraft.
  • KLM Royal Dutch Airlines was identified as the customer for two A321neo aircraft.

In November and December, Airbus booked meaningful orders from Chinese airlines and lessors for the Airbus A320neo family while we also saw good order inflow for the Airbus A350 and Airbus A330neo. The company booked a total of 278 orders while orders for 12 airplanes were cancelled, leaving 264 orders valued at $23.6 billion. In the same months last year, the company received 129 gross orders and 96 net orders valued at $6.8 billion.

For the entire year, Airbus received 1,000 gross orders and 111 cancellations, bringing the net orders to 889 orders valued at $75.9 billion. In 2024, net orders were 826, valued at $66.5 billion. So, we saw a 7.6% increase in net orders and a 14% increase in net order value.

Airbus Meets Revised Delivery Target

In November and December, Airbus delivered a total of 208 airplanes valued at $13.7 billion:

  • Airbus delivered 27 Airbus A220s.
  • A total of 151 Airbus A320neo family airplanes were delivered, including three A319neos, 48 A320neos, and 100 Airbus A321neos.
  • Fourteen Airbus A330 airplanes were delivered, including one Airbus A330-200 for multi-role tanker transport configuration, one Airbus A330-800, and 11 Airbus A330-900s.
  • Airbus delivered 17 Airbus A350s, including 11 -900s and six -1000s.

For Airbus, the final two months were all about catching up on deliveries. The company had expected a strong year, but had to revise its deliveries downward for the full year and in the delivery volumes in the final two months of the year. We also see that the company delivered just one airplane more than in the final two months of the year.

For 2025, the company delivered 793 airplanes valued at $50.6 billion, which is in line with the delivery target for approximately 790 airplanes but substantially lower than the 820 airplanes that it had targeted initially. In 2024, the company delivered 766 airplanes valued at $49 billion. In my view, the company’s full-year performance was underwhelming. Especially with the problems at Boeing, the general impression is that Airbus is doing a whole lot better in terms of aligning targets with supply chain health. The opposite is true, and we see that the company has been running behind on the delivery ramp-up for several years now, forcing it to revise delivery targets downward.

The book-to-bill ratio during November and December was 1.3x in terms of units and 1.8x in terms of value. For the full year, those ratios were 1.3x and 1.6x.

Conclusion: Airbus Is Failing To Meet Its Own Targets

On airplane orders, we are seeing that demand for Airbus airplanes remains strong, and those airplane orders provide continued support for higher production levels. That, however, is exactly what the problem has become at Airbus. Post-pandemic, Airbus has put out very aggressive delivery targets, but the reality is that it has been missing delivery targets with the exception of 2023. I definitely believe that Airbus has a strong product portfolio, but in recent years we have seen the company setting targets that pressure suppliers and lead to the company missing delivery targets, and that is definitely something where the company has to improve.

data.title: Steel Dynamics: Free Cash Flow Inflecting, But Valuation Is Full
data.description:
Shares of Steel Dynamics, Inc. (STLD) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining 50%. While tariffs initially had a mixed impact on its business, we are increasingly seeing the benefits of steel tariffs protecting domestic steelmakers like STLD, and with its key aluminum growth project ramping up, investor excitement about potential capital returns has increased.

I last covered shares of Steel Dynamics in October, downgrading them to a Hold, which was an overly cautious stance. The stock is up 27% since then, so I should have recommended investors continue to buy shares. With updated financials and a clarifying macro picture for 2026, now is a good time to revisit STLD and determine how to position for the year ahead.

Tariffs lift steel results, despite a mixed demand environment

In the company’s fourth quarter, Steel Dynamics earned $1.82, which beat estimates by $0.12 as revenue increased 14% to $4.4 billion. The company generated $505 million of EBITDA. This was up from $372 million last year, primarily thanks to improvements in its steel operations. Its average steel selling price was up 9% to $1,107, and volumes increased by 9% to 3.3 million tons. While the sector struggled earlier this year with excess foreign supply as importers front-ran tariffs, steel tariffs are now more clearly restricting supply, helping companies like Steel Dynamics raise prices and gain market share. Indeed, steel production hit a record high last year.

Drilling into segment results, steel operations made $322 million in operating income, nearly double last year’s $165 million. Results were weighed down by the fact that planned maintenance took a bit longer to complete, reducing output by about 150,000 tons. Activity typically slows in Q4, given seasonally lower construction activity, so STLD plans more maintenance in Q4. Given seasonal pressures, operating income fell by 35% sequentially. I view this drop as primarily seasonal and not a cause for concern. I was encouraged that management saw solid demand from the nonresidential construction sector, an area of concern for me.

As you can see below, nonresidential construction, the leading use for steel, has languished over the past 2.5 years after a strong run. Elevated interest rates have weakened the infrastructure spending cycle (which is increasingly being held up by the less steel-intensive data center subsector). This has created a difficult demand environment for STLD and its peers. In 2026, I expect nonresidential construction spending to remain muted, being flat to up 2%, as there should be some benefit from factory spending, though real government spending is likely to be a bit softer. Even with tariffs restricting supply, this will temper potential steel price increases. I also remain cautious on demand from the auto sector, given tariffs and consumers shying away from discretionary big-ticket purchases.

Steel fabrication profits, conversely, were down 36% to $91 million. This unit provides more value-added products, rather than just commodity steel. As a result, it has a much higher margin. Shipments are only about 4% of total steel shipments, but they are about 20% of profits. Volumes were down 5% from last year, and the company faced some seasonal pressure. I expect this to represent a bottom in results as orders were strong, led by demand from data centers and manufacturers. As these projects ramp up and STLD executes on its backlog, we should see profitability improve.

Aluminum ramp supports increased cash flow

Its aluminum segment generated a $47 million loss, better than the $57 million loss last quarter, as the Mississippi growth facility continues to ramp up. This $2.5 billion project began producing aluminum in June 2025, but it will take several quarters before it is fully operational, during which time it will be loss-making (given the fixed cost of running a facility). I expect it to flip to breakeven before year-end and be a clear contributor in 2027. This facility should help diversify the company’s cash flow and be less cyclical given its focus on providing aluminum for beverage cans, though its auto exposure will be more cyclical.

During 2025, STLD generated $1.2 billion of free cash flow, up from $626 million last year, primarily due to the normalization of cap-ex after an investment super-cycle. Cap-ex was down nearly half in 2025 to $950 million from $1.87 billion in 2024. I expect cap-ex to continue to ramp down, boosting free cash flow; however, STLD may replace this with inorganic activity. Earlier this month, it disclosed it was part of a consortium to acquire BlueScope Steel. Under the proposed terms, SGH would buy BlueScope Steel for $8.8 billion and then sell its U.S. operations to STLD for cash. BlueScope has rejected this bid, and a deal is highly uncertain. While the outcome is unknown, I do not expect a deal to be completed. Nevertheless, it points to STLD having the appetite to add assets and continue to grow.

Importantly, the company maintains a solid balance sheet. Leverage has ticked up over the past few years as EBITDA has come down from extremely elevated levels post-COVID. Additionally, STLD has increased cap-ex via its aluminum plant while also returning cash to investors via buybacks. Its balance sheet was stronger than necessary in 2022-2023, with STLD carrying essentially no net debt. Leverage is not at 1.5x, and I would like to see the company maintain leverage in the 1-2x range, consistent with where we are now. As such, I expect capital returns to come out of free cash flow rather than be debt-financed. It has no 2026 maturities.

Steel Dynamics focuses most of its capital returns on share buybacks, and it repurchased $240 million of stock in Q4. For the year, STLD bought back about 4% of shares. Additionally, shares offer a 1.1% dividend yield. As you can see below, Steel Dynamics has steadily grown its dividend. The pace of buybacks has normalized as free cash flow has come down from unsustainably high levels. Still, these repurchases have reduced about a third of its share count. While I expect further dividend growth, the trajectory of buybacks is a bit less clear, depending on whether STLD successfully acquires the BlueScope assets.

Shares are a hold

Back in October, I saw Steel Dynamics having ~$12.50 in mid-cycle earnings power as its aluminum facility ramped up fully. After earning ~$8.00 in 2025, I expect STLD to generate this mid-cycle ~$12.50 in 2026, +/-$0.50 as the steel market has tightened considerably and its aluminum facility reaches run-rate. Consensus for 2026 is $13.39, which is a bit above my estimate. My relative caution comes from my expectation that demand growth will be limited, given the fairly stagnant backdrop for nonresidential construction, which I do not expect to materially change over the next 6-12 months.

At ~$182, STLD is trading at about 14.5x earnings, and this is a fairly full multiple for a commodity company even as its cap-ex is coming down. That said, with many stocks trading over 20x and with the steel market having tightened, it is not an extremely expensive valuation, and it is possible that tariffs continue to improve steel market dynamics and push earnings beyond mid-cycle levels, leaving my EPS forecast a bit conservative.

As such, I do not see a pressing need to sell shares here, especially as buybacks could increase, assuming no M&A. While I see shares around fair value, these potential upside risks, as well as STLD’s strong balance sheet and operating track record, keep me a Hold. However, I would not rush to buy at this valuation and would like a pullback below $170 before buying.

data.title: Bandwidth: Software Growth Set To Drive Margin Expansion Ahead
data.description:
In recent quarters, I have observed Bandwidth (BAND) steadily improving its underlying business fundamentals, with healthy top-line growth and expanding margins. This view has supported my Buy rating on the stock. The company is effectively tapping into the global voice AI market by offering software services like transcription, translation, and voice cloning, thereby generating a fast-growing stream of high-margin recurring revenue. In fact, BAND's CEO has previously stated that an AI voice call with these added features could generate between three and four times as much revenue as that of a standard voice call. Following 8% underlying cloud communications revenue growth expected for FY25, I see growth accelerating in 2026, helped by political messaging from the upcoming US midterm elections. Given the tailwinds from AI and the growing contribution from software revenue, I see BAND's moat strengthening, justifying a significantly higher multiple than its current 5.2x FCF. I am therefore maintaining my Buy rating.

Outlook for 2026

Excluding the contribution from political messaging related to the US Presidential election in 2024, Q3 cloud communications revenue was up 8% year-over-year. Based on management's guidance, Q4 cloud communications revenue growth is set to accelerate modestly to 10%. Notably, I chose to exclude messaging surcharges from my estimates, as they only represent pass-through costs. NRR was compressed due to the lower political contribution but remained healthy at 107% when adjusting for this. It remains a key metric to track for this business in order to understand existing customer adoption of BAND's growing voice AI product offering.

The Communication Platform as a Service (“CPaaS”) market is forecasted to grow at an 18.8% CAGR from 2025 to 2034, with AI-powered chatbots and virtual assistants being a key growth driver. BAND is leveraging its Maestro software to provide customers with the infrastructure necessary to run their AI applications with their respective Unified Communications as a Service (“UCaaS”) and Contact Center as a Service (“CCaaS”) providers. While BAND typically earns revenue based on the usage of its platform for calls and messages, Maestro now brings in a software platform fee as a high-margin revenue source for the company. Software revenue is expected to reach an annual revenue run rate of $10 million when exiting 2025, according to commentary by the CFO during the Q3 call.

While this is still a small fraction of overall revenue, it is important to recognize that this comes with high gross margins and flows directly through to the bottom line. Assuming a 70% gross margin attached to this software revenue, it would generate roughly $7 million, or about 7% of FY25 adjusted EBITDA of $91 million. In addition to the AI features that BAND continues to add to its platform, it is also introducing other features like Number Reputation Management. This not only drives software revenue growth but also increases product stickiness with customers.

When it comes to cloud communications revenue growth for 2026, I see underlying year-over-year growth of at least 8% over the $557 million expected for 2025. I also include a political messaging contribution of $20 million, which is slightly lower than the $23 million contribution from the 2024 Presidential election. This translates to FY26 cloud communications revenue of $624 million, up 12% year-over-year. I expect the growing mix of software revenue coupled with the political messaging contribution to lift adjusted gross margins from 58% to above 60% for the first time, powering strong bottom-line growth.

Cheap Valuation at 5.2x FCF Offers Downside Support

Based on my expectation for cloud communication revenue to reach $624 million in FY26, with adjusted gross margins just over 60%, I see adjusted EBITDA landing at $120 million. This includes approximately $12 million in gross profit from political messaging flowing directly to the bottom-line, helping lift adjusted EBITDA margins to 19%, compared to 16% expected for FY25. Assuming $30 million in investment capex and $5 million in tax expenses, I estimate FY26 FCF of $85 million. This implies a 13.3% FCF margin, which is slightly less than management's earlier target of reaching a 15% margin set at the 2023 Investor Day.

BAND's current market cap stands at $440 million, valued at just 5.2 times FCF. Given the growth outlook and margin potential I see ahead, this appears very cheap. The market is currently rewarding a front-end solution provider like Twilio (TWLO) over a back-end infrastructure provider like BAND. Despite demonstrating similar growth rates and margins, TWLO stock trades at close to 20 times FCF. The valuation gap here appears too large and should converge as the percentage mix of BAND's software revenue keeps growing.

While convertible debt on its balance sheet has been a concern of mine, this is now diminishing as FCF ramps higher. The $175 million in net debt on the balance sheet seems manageable at roughly two times the annual FCF expected, especially with the convertible notes only maturing in April 2028. With further deleveraging expected ahead, this gives management the flexibility for the first time to even consider returning capital to shareholders through share buybacks.

As I have done previously, I want to point out that stock-based compensation has been on the rise, now at around $50 million annually, as shown above. Shares outstanding have increased at a 9% CAGR since 2024, though this rate should drastically decrease in the coming quarters given that the number of non-vested restricted stock units issued and outstanding was down 30% in Q3 compared to Q4 2024. Nevertheless, dilution is something I will pay close attention to, expecting to see it return to historical levels in the low single digits.

BAND Stock Remains a Buy

BAND's stock price has continued to remain disconnected from the underlying business, which is seeing improving prospects for growth supported by industry tailwinds and margin expansion. I believe growing software revenue from AI voice services will help in closing this valuation disconnect. Political messaging adds further upside to this year's estimates, and at 5.2x FCF, the stock is just too cheap. The risk-reward setup appears attractive, and I am therefore maintaining my Buy rating.

data.title: Microsoft Hit My Price Target And Pulled Back: Why I'm Upgrading To Strong Buy
data.description:
Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) has underperformed since my last report, returning 9.5% against the S&P 500’s (SP500) 23.4%. That is not the type of performance I am looking for in buy-rated names. However, it should be noted that the stock price reached a 52-week high of $555.45 against my price target of $502.86. So, MSFT's stock price performed above expectations. In this report, I will analyze the stock price performance, discuss the expectations for Q2 2026, assess the risks and opportunities for Microsoft, and update my price target.

A Look At MSFT Stock Performance

I believe it is always good to not solely look at the share price development from the time of the publication until the time we revisit a company’s stock. The reason is quite simple, and that reason is that it may ignore that a price target has been reached between those two points in time. I initially saw around 27% upside with a $502 price target, which was reached by the 9th of July 2025. If we look at the price performance of the S&P 500 in the same time period, we note that the S&P 500 returned “only” 12.5%.

Furthermore, if we stretch the period until the point where Microsoft stock reached its all-time high, which was towards the end of October 2025, we note that the stock delivered a 37.1% return compared to the 23.7% return for the S&P 500. So, the stock actually delivered and then some, while it still outperformed the S&P 500. That also means that I am not unhappy with the buy rating and the share price performance.

Wall Street Expects 21% Growth in EPS and 15% Growth in Sales

For the second quarter, analysts are expecting Microsoft to report revenues of $80.3 billion, indicating 15.3% growth. Estimates range from $78.6 billion to $81.7 billion. Revisions on revenue expectations have barely changed over the past months, with a 0.4% upward revision over the past six months and less than 0.1% over the past three months. We note that 20 analysts revised estimates upward while 16 analysts revised estimates down.

On earnings per share, analysts expect $3.92, indicating 21.3% growth with estimates ranging from $3.56 to $4.12. Over the past six months, revisions were up 3.3% and stable over the past three months, with 26 upward revisions and three downward EPS revisions.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For Microsoft?

The big opportunity for Microsoft is rather straightforward: the growth of cloud and artificial intelligence. However, that also immediately highlights the risks for the business.

The major risks for Microsoft center on AI. Currently, many companies are pouring money into AI capital expenditures and rolling out AI functionalities. On the surface, that seems like a very good decision. However, we note that the heavy investments right now also mean that AI top-line growth comes at the expense of gross margin compression. We’re also seeing that regulatory scrutiny on AI is increasing. Now, I do not see an immediate impact for Microsoft on that end, but it is definitely something to keep in mind.

Another risk is that Microsoft may be too focused on AI growth. I use Azure for storage and to run some data flows. The image that you find below in this article is actually not just an image; it is the screenshot of our evoX Stock Screener developed with Power BI. Power BI is a great tool to visualize data, but a lot of work needs to be done to provide users with the flexibility to visualize things the way they want. Over the past year, frustration among users has been growing. Power BI gets monthly updates, and what I noticed is that the Power BI team would direct a whole lot of resources to inject Copilot, which Microsoft sees as its AI assistant. Reality, however, is that very few users, including small business users like myself, are looking for AI solutions. That also means that what users are looking for in terms of improvements and updates diverged substantially from what Microsoft is rolling out.

I consider that to be an obsession with AI without a clear use case for many users. That is not to say that AI has no use case, because it definitely has. However, Microsoft largely disregarded the need for non-AI updates and investments, while a significant share of growth still comes from non-AI.

In my prior report, around half of the Azure growth was driven by non-AI growth. In the most recent quarter, non-AI growth in Azure was better than expected, while non-AI growth in Personal Computing and Productivity and Business Processes was largely in line with segment growth. That also shows that near-term investments are significant in AI, but at this moment, it doesn’t drive above non-AI growth. So, the risk is that the company invests heavily in tomorrow’s AI injection and use cases while largely disregarding existing growth in non-AI.

Expectations for AI are definitely high, and that justifies the investments made today. However, in some way, Microsoft seems to be directing efforts away from non-AI growth, which increases the pressure on AI growth to materialize and feed to the bottom line and into the margin expansion line.

Microsoft Upgraded To Strong Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

While I am definitely not a big fan of the company’s apparent neglect of non-AI investment and growth, I am upgrading the stock to a strong buy. EBITDA was 6.8% higher in 2025 than estimated, setting the stage for upward revisions for 2026 and 2027 to the tune of 15%. On free cash flow, the company performed around 7.3% better than expected. However, driven by higher capital expenditures, estimates for 2026 and 2027 came down by 7% and 18%. What makes the stock a strong buy in my view is that it has upside against the current year's expected earnings, while it has 34% upside against FY27 earnings. As a result, I am marking the stock a strong buy with a $624.43 price target.

In terms of margin performance, I expect the company to continue its growth trajectory. That growth in EBITDA margin is not solely AI growth but also non-AI growth. Cash conversion is likely going to be somewhat pressured as capital expenditures accelerate to 53%-58% of operating cash flow. We note that operating cash flow is expected to grow linearly, but free cash flow will remain at a relatively lower level as capital expenditures continue to ramp up. In terms of cash and debt, Microsoft is facing absolutely no issues with a net-debt level that is likely to turn into a net cash position and a cash balance that could grow to $156 billion by 2028.

Conclusion: Microsoft Is Looking Attractive

While I am not a big fan of Microsoft’s obsession with injecting AI even where it is not desired, the company’s stock is looking attractive as we are seeing growth in core non-AI, and while having a lower margin, we also see growth in AI. That ultimately positions the company to grow margins in AI and attempt what it also attempted with cloud and cloud computing, and that is building market dominance. As a result, I am upgrading MSFT stock to Strong Buy on attractive growth and valuation.

data.title: Japanese Lenders Likely To Grow Margins; Asia-Pacific Peers Face Compression
data.description:
Japanese megabanks could increase their net interest margins over the next two years, diverging from a trend across Asia-Pacific, as the nation's central bank is expected to further raise interest rates.

All four Japan-based lenders among the 25 largest banks by assets in the region are projected to post gains in net interest margin (NIM) in calendar years 2026 and 2027, according to Visible Alpha estimates.

Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group Inc. (MUFG), Japan's biggest lender by assets, is projected to increase its NIM to 0.77% in 2026 and to 0.81% in 2027 from an estimated 0.72% in 2025. Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group Inc. (SMFG) and Mizuho Financial Group Inc. (MFG) are expected to see a roughly 20-basis point increase in their NIMs between 2023 and 2027, while that of Japan Post Bank Co. Ltd. (JPSTF) could more than double during the period.

"As long as the Bank of Japan (BOJ) raises interest rates, the banks' NIMs will continue to rise," said Toyoki Sameshima, a senior analyst at SBI Securities Co. "Their NIMs overseas may shrink if the Federal Reserve moves on a rate cut path," Sameshima said, adding, "but overall, the NIMs should improve as their domestic lending surpasses overseas loans."

The Japan way

The BOJ abandoned its negative interest rate policy by raising its short-term policy rate to between zero and 0.1% from minus 0.1% in March 2024. It followed with further rate increases, taking the benchmark rate to about 0.75% by December 2025, the highest in three decades.

Sameshima expects the BOJ to raise its policy rate again as early as July and then to increase it every six months, with rates eventually reaching a terminal level of 1.25% to 1.5% over the next few years.

However, the timing of the BOJ's next move has been muddled by the Japanese yen's steady decline against the US dollar since April 2025; a spike in local bond yields, with the 10-year government bond touching a 27-year high on Jan. 20; and Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi calling for snap elections to be held in February.

"The outcome of the election, subsequent fiscal policy management and the reaction in financial markets could create fresh uncertainties for the BOJ's outlook," Nomura said in a Jan. 15 note. In line with Nomura's expectation, the central bank left its policy rate unchanged at its Jan. 22–23 meeting. The brokerage estimates the BOJ will "pause in 2026, followed by two more hikes in 2027," with a weaker yen being "a risk."

The rest

On the other hand, most major central banks in Asia-Pacific have been easing monetary policy as inflation has subsided across the region and post-pandemic growth momentum has weakened. The cycle was set in motion when the US Federal Reserve announced a 50-basis point rate cut in September 2024, saying "the time has come for policy to adjust."

Falling policy rates weighed on banks' NIMs as lenders had to pass on rate cuts to borrowers to remain competitive. Deposits are also getting repriced, though with a lag. The median NIM for the region's 25 largest banks is estimated to narrow by 6 basis points in 2025 to 1.34%, Visible Alpha data shows.

Nomura expects the easing cycle across Asia to be "largely complete, despite low inflation, reflecting improving growth, near-neutral policy rates and the need to conserve ammunition," according to a recent note. Analysts, however, see "a North-South monetary policy divide forming, with an extended hold in [South] Korea, New Zealand and Australia, but 50-75 bps of further rate cuts in the South (Thailand, India, Indonesia and the Philippines)."

The aftereffects of the easing cycle are expected to sustain pressure on margins for at least the next two years, with NIMs projected to stabilize at 1.29% in 2027 for banks in the sample, according to consensus estimates on Visible Alpha.

Chinese banks

The People's Bank of China (PBoC) held its one- and five-year loan prime rates steady on Jan. 20, though the central bank has recently used other easing tools. The government reported on Jan. 19 that gross domestic product grew 4.5% year over year in the October–December 2025 period, the slowest expansion in three years. The world's second-largest economy, however, achieved its full-year growth target of about 5%.

There remains scope for cuts to the policy rate and the reverse repo rate in 2026, PBoC Deputy Governor Zou Lan said in a Jan. 15 news conference, citing stabilizing bank NIMs and an improving foreign exchange environment.

The PBoC announced a targeted easing package, including a new 1 trillion yuan program for private enterprises. The PBoC also cut interest rates on all structural and relending facilities to help reduce banks' funding costs, stabilize margins and create room for further rate cuts, according to Zou.

"The struggling property market and weakening retail credit demands have yet to see signs of recovery in 2026," said Iris Tan, senior equity analyst at Morningstar. Further margin headwinds are expected in 2026 and beyond due to an unfavorable shift in the loan mix toward a lower share of high-yielding retail loans, Tan said.

NIMs at most of the 15 Chinese banks on the list are expected to be lower in 2026 before stabilizing in 2027. Bank of China Ltd.'s (BACHF) NIM is projected to ease by 3 bps in 2026 to 1.16%, before stabilizing at 1.15% in 2027, while Agricultural Bank of China (ACGBY) is expected to post a 4-bps contraction to 1.23% in 2026 before levelling off at 1.21% in 2027, the data shows.

As of Jan. 23, US$1 was equivalent to 6.96 Chinese yuan.

Visible Alpha is a part of S&P Global Market Intelligence.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
